summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where the parties were married for approximately 17 years – Where these proceedings now have clarity as to the pool of interests and tax liabilities which may arise as a result of Orders made by this Court – Final orders made to achieve justice and equity for both parties

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BAUMANN J:

INTRODUCTION

These property proceedings between the Applicant wife, Ms Sowards (“the wife”) (now aged 54 years) and the Respondent husband, Mr Sowards (“the husband”) (now aged 65 years), have been delayed initially by the complications that arose from the husband’s failure to file any required income tax returns for over 20 years. This failure meant crystallising the tax liabilities for him personally and entities controlled by him (associated with his activities as a property developer) was a real impediment to finalising the litigation initially commenced by the wife on 13 July 2021.
As required, a history, succinctly stated, is set out later in these Reasons, however an important interim property Order was made on 6 December 2023, as explained by the Reasons for Judgment published at that time (see Sowards & Sowards [2023] FedCFamC1F 1039).
The Orders made at that time were in these terms:
That by 4.00pm on 13 December 2023, the husband shall pay the wife the sum of $700,000, and if that sum is not paid as ordered, the wife have liberty to re-list the matter for further hearing at 9.30am on 14 December 2023.
That the wife retain absolutely to the exclusion of the husband her interest in:
the property held in her name situated at [B Street], [Suburb D] in the state of Queensland more particularly described as [1 B Street] and [2 B Street] on [redacted] on Title Reference [redacted];
b. her member entitlements with [Superannuation Fund 1];

c. the share portfolio;

d. the [E] Life Insurance policy in her name;

e. her bank accounts; and
the furniture, contents, jewellery and other personal chattels in her possession and control as at the date of these Orders.
3. That the husband retain absolutely to the exclusion of the wife his interest in:
a. his member entitlements with [Superannuation Fund 2];

b. his bank accounts;

c. the [F] Life Insurance policy in his name;

d. his motor vehicle;

e. his [motor vehicle 2]; and
the furniture, contents and other personal chattels in his possession and control as at the date of these Orders (save for his interest in [G] Pty Ltd, [H] Pty Ltd and [J] Pty Ltd).
That in the event that the wife and the husband or either of them fails, refuses or neglects to execute any documents or to anything necessary to give effect to these Orders, then pursuant to s 106A of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), a Registrar of this Court is hereby appointed to execute any deed, document or instrument in the name of the party, and do all acts and things necessary to give validity to the operation of the deed, document or instrument. It is deemed that there is a failure or neglect to sign if the party to whom the documents are being submitted has not signed them within the time frame specified in these Orders, and the affidavit of the party not in default testifying to this breach is deemed sufficient evidence of the breach.
...
That pursuant to r 3.06 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth), the wife shall within seven (7) days notify the Australian Taxation Office (“ATO”) of these proceedings and of their right to seek to intervene in these proceedings.
That pursuant to s 121 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), the wife have leave to provide to the ATO a copy of:
a. the Reasons for Judgment delivered 6 December 2023;

b. these Orders; and

c. her trial affidavit filed 14 November 2023.
That the husband personally and as a Director of [G] Pty Ltd be restrained and an injunction issue restraining him from selling, transferring or encumbering his interest in the property at [K Street], [Suburb L].
That by 8 February 2024, the husband file and serve an affidavit setting out any further actions taken to crystalise his taxation obligations.
That these proceedings be adjourned for Case Management Hearing at 9.30am on 15 February 2024 in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1) at Brisbane.
At the time of this Judgment, a notional pool of $6,407,655 was determined (see [22]). The husband complied with those Orders by paying to the wife the required payment of $700,000. As the Orders made clear, the wife was required to notify the Australian Taxation Office (“ATO”) of their right to intervene.
Although by final submissions most entries of the pool of interests were agreed, the differences and contested entries have some real significance. The reasons which follow seek to explain what orders achieve both finality and justice and equity.

CONTEXTUAL HISTORY

Statements of fact hereafter should be construed as findings of fact.
The parties’ relationship spans over some 17 years, with cohabitation commencing upon their marriage in early 2000, albeit noting that the parties commenced their relationship in about 1993 when they were 23 and 33 years old respectively.
Early into their relationship in early 1994, both the husband and wife independently purchased their own properties, being:
B Street, Suburb D by the wife for $205,000; and
GG Street, Suburb L, by the husband for $295,000.
In November 1994, the husband also purchased a property at K Street, Suburb L through his entity – G Pty Ltd.
In 2000, the parties married and over the coming years were blessed with the birth of their three children:
(a) Ms CC – in 2002;

(b) MR C – in 2004; and

(c) X – in 2009.

During this time the husband continued working as a property developer (within his own company) and the wife undertook the role of homemaker, whilst also maintaining periods of employment as a health worker in between the births of the three children.
The husband was successful as a property developer but also had access to funds and resources arising from the Sowards Family Trust, that had been established by the husband’s father Mr EE, with a Trustee controlled by him called J Pty Ltd. These entities were created in the 1970s through which a family livestock station in Queensland operated.
In 1983, J Pty Ltd purchased properties in Brisbane at R Street, Suburb S (“the [Suburb S] property”) and HH Street, Suburb JJ (“the [Suburb JJ] property”).
In 1986, the husband’s father passed away, and the husband says (at paragraph 35 of his affidavit), and I accept as accurate, that:
The Will made provisions for, among other things:
Our family sheep and cattle grazing property known as “[J]” and livestock to be gifted to me but I was to be responsible for the property’s debts and for purchasing a property for my sister [Ms KK Sowards]; and
[Three] Brisbane properties... to be gifted to my Mother;
For me to receive a share of my Father’s cash deposits; and
For me to receive my Father’s shares and personal effects.

(As per the original)

The husband was approximately 29 years old when his father died, and in circumstances where assets were vested in the Trust, no actual transfers were required.
After his father’s death, the husband and his mother Mrs O (who is now 85 years of age) became the only directors of J Pty Ltd. As various properties were acquired by the Family Trust before the implementation of Capital Gains Tax (“CGT”), retaining the assets in the Trust was prudent.
In 1989, the homestead upon the J property sadly burnt down, with the husband receiving $200,000 under an insurance claim. This was the catalyst for the property being sold in around 1990 for $1,600,000 – a sizeable sum in those days. As already noted, the vehicle holding the funds (being the Family Trust) was used – from which the husband allocated approximately $400,000 to purchase a home for his sister Ms KK, consistent with his late father’s wishes, but vested for Ms KK into a protective trust.
Although these transactions all took place before the parties began a relationship in 1993, when the husband’s mother Mrs O purchased the property P Street, Suburb D (“the [P Street] property”) in February 1991, then vacant land upon which a home was constructed with funds from the family Trust (including insurance proceeds), its existence and subsequent residence by the husband and wife for seven years makes the circumstances of its acquisition contextually relevant.
Ultimately, the wife at the final hearing did not challenge the assertion of Mrs O that the husband has no legal interest in the P Street property, but Mrs O was briefly cross-examined on the evidence remaining (after objection) contained in her affidavit filed 17 October 2024, particularly about her “intentions in relation to the [P Street] property and my Estate generally” set out at paragraphs 79 to 109. I refer to this evidence when discussing relevant s 75(2) factors below.
From this early history and subsequent events, it is clear that a degree of “intermingling” of funds in both the Sowards Family Trust and the husband’s entities H Pty Ltd and G Pty Ltd have taken place – including the evidence of Mrs O at paragraphs 110 to 118 of her affidavit, relating to her financial support from the Family Trust, all arranged by the husband. Limited financial statements and income tax returns for the husband’s entities were produced to the Court – although at annexure “I” to the husband’s trial affidavit (by reference to paragraph 320), the husband produced his personal tax account ledger as at 12 August 2024 and Notices of Assessment of Income Tax for:
(a) G Pty Ltd for the financial years 2001 to 2023; and

(b) H Pty Ltd for the financial years 2006 to 2023.

As I will note when discussing the pool of interests, the agreement reached by the experienced Counsel reflected in most of Exhibit 4, relieves the Court of the need to unravel the likely numerous interparty transactions between the husband, his mother Mrs O and the various entities. Also, it is not necessary to analyse the financial effect of the husband’s property development of “[MM property]” which occurred with third parties between 2004 to 2006, and were all sold by 2017.
As noted, the family moved into the P Street property in 2007, until the wife left the home with the children. The evidence reveals that the separation was unsettling for the children, who were aged approximately 15, 12 and 8 years respectively at the time of separation. A divorce was granted in early 2021.
Post separation, the wife undertook major repairs to her B Street, Suburb D property. In his evidence, the husband did not dispute the wife’s estimate that the costs of renovation were approximately $880,000 – all financed by way of loans to the wife from her mother and subsequently reflected in a Loan Agreement dated 27 October 2020 (annexure “TAB10”) to the wife’s affidavit.
That loan had increased to $1,079,704 by the time of the hearing – increasing because of the wife’s mother assisting with legal expenses and school and children’s expenses (see paragraph 128).
A truncated procedural history also for context follows:
(a) On 11 March 2022, a Senior Judicial Registrar noted that the husband and the entities within the “[Sowards] Group” had not filed any tax returns since 2000;

(b) On 3 November 2022, a Judicial Registrar ordered that:

The husband file and serve, by no later than 11 November 2022, his Response to the Initiating Application filed on 13 July 2021.
The husband contact the Australian Taxation Office ("ATO"), either in his personal capacity or through his taxation solicitors, at least once per month, in the first week of each month beginning in November 2022, until such time as he receives a substantive response from the ATO as to the steps he is required to take to remedy the issues identified.
The husband provide a copy of all correspondence passing between himself/his lawyers and the ATO within 48 hours of sending or receiving that correspondence.
And further noted that:
The Husband was ordered to file his response material on 22 September 2021, 11 March 2022 and 5 May 2022. He appears to have attempted to file it in May 2022 but it has not yet been filed.
The husband has advised the ATO on 2 August 2022 that there are some tax returns outstanding but is waiting advice from the ATO as to what steps he needs to take to remedy those outstanding returns.
Once the husband's taxation affairs are brought into order the parties intend to attend mediation.
(c) The husband filed his first Response in the proceedings on 7 December 2022 – being over one year since the wife commenced proceedings.
With growing frustration reasonably exhibited by the wife and her legal team arising from the husband’s failure to finalise his tax affairs, on 7 September 2023 I listed the matter for a hearing before me for three days commencing 4 December 2023.
I rely upon my published Reasons for Judgment (see Sowards & Sowards [2023] FedCFamC1F 1039), and what some might consider my unusual decision to make partial property orders – even when the husband was appearing at the hearing without legal representation. The earlier Orders set out in those Reasons were based essentially on:
(a) my refusal to grant an adjournment;

(b) some assumed valuations at that time being:

(i) the value of two shares held by the husband in G Pty Ltd at $1,847,046 a share;

(ii) the value of the husband’s nine (out of 10) shares in H Pty Ltd at $1,725,745;

(iii) the value of two (out of four) shares in J Pty Ltd at $257,359;

(iv) the husband’s personal share portfolios of $1,381,747;

(v) the wife’s property at B Street Suburb D at $2,500,000;

(vi) various smaller items set out at paragraph 22 of those earlier Reasons;

(vii) a loan owed by the wife to her mother of $881,204;

(viii) allowance for “potential future taxation liabilities of the husband of $1,000,000;

(ix) a total superannuation benefit of $374,839 (with the wife having an interest of $341,839); and

(x) certain unquantified “financial resources” in the form of the wife’s interest in the Estate of [Mr N] and the husband’s benefit from the [P Street] property.

At [28] to [38] of my earlier Reasons, I expressed my basis for making the partial property Orders at that time – but making it clear when a final hearing took place, the pool of interests would need to be reviewed. It was also made clear (as set out at [34]) that by making a determination of the wife’s entitlement of not less than 43% of what was still a very uncertain pool of interests, “this is not to be construed as a determination that the wife’s claim of 55% or the husband’s claim that the wife will only receive 40% is not arguable”.
On 15 February 2024, the matter returned before the Court, and a solicitor Mr Olthof appeared on behalf of the ATO who informed the Court that (at that point in time) the ATO did not have a definite decision as to whether they intended to formally intervene in the proceedings. I informed the solicitor that it was my position that the ATO should provide a formal answer as to whether the ATO intended on intervening. Accordingly, I ordered the husband to file and serve an amended Response and that he serve a copy of such Response along with a copy of his affidavit filed 8 February 2024 upon the Commissioner.
When the matter returned before me on 16 May 2024, two interim issues had arisen being:
(a) An Application in a Proceeding filed by subpoena objector on 18 April 2024; and

(b) An Application in a Proceeding filed by the husband’s former legal representative, Simonidis Steel on 13 May 2024.

The Application in a Proceeding filed on 18 April 2024 was referred by me to a Judicial Registrar for hearing. Such Application was ultimately resolved by the Judicial Registrar on 28 August 2024, with a further Order being made for costs on 3 October 2024:
By resort to r 12.17(1)(a) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth), the Respondent (“issuing party”) must pay [Ms U] (“Non Party Objector”) costs of and incidental to the subpoena objection hearing fixed in the sum of $3,000, by no later than 4.00pm on 27 November 2024.
The Application in a Proceeding filed on 13 May 2024 sought the following orders:
In the event that it is declared that [BB] Pty Ltd is not a party entitled as of right to intervene pursuant to section 79(10)(a) of the Family Law Act 1975, then in the alternative, pursuant to section 92 of the Act, it is ordered that leave be granted for [BB] Pty Ltd to intervene in these proceedings.
That from any final award of property settlement that may be awarded to the First Respondent by this Court (after payment of any and all costs the first respondent may be ordered to pay to the second respondent) the amount of $8934.88 be paid in priority to [BB Pty Ltd] with any remaining balance funds then being paid to the First Respondent.
That the husband pay the costs of [BB Pty Ltd] of an incidental to this Application pursuant to the Family Law scale, such costs being paid in priority to the First Respondent receiving any property settlement award this Court may award him.

(As per the original)

Such Application was listed to the first day of the final hearing on 6 November 2024. However, I now note that as BB Pty Ltd filed a Notice of Discontinuance on 9 April 2025, I do not intend to deal with that Application any further.
By 18 July 2024, the solicitor appearing on behalf of the ATO (as an interested party) informed the Court that the Commissioner was in the process of issuing an assessment. Having informed the parties that I did not wish to make any further directions until the position of the ATO was clear, I adjourned the proceedings to a date in September 2024.
On 3 September 2024, the matter returned before the Court, where I was informed by Mr Metlej of Counsel for the ATO that:
(a) an assessment had been made by the Commissioner;

(b) the husband personally had no tax liability assigned to him, however his company G Pty Ltd had received an assessment of a liability of approximately $690,000 to be paid by a date later that month; and

(c) the ATO no longer sought to be heard, nor did they propose to intervene in these proceedings.

With this longstanding taxation element now resolved, I set the matter down for a final hearing to commence on 6 November 2024, with experienced members of the Bar, Ms Horsley appearing on behalf of the wife, and Mr Alexander (as he then was) appearing on behalf of the husband.

STATUTORY FRAMEWORK

Shortly stated, but more concisely and elaborately described in the Full Court decision in Hickey & Hickey [2003] FamCA 395; (2003) FLC 93-143, in a property settlement case, the Court must adopt a well-known four-step process, essentially:
(a) to identify the pool of assets and liabilities generally, and usually at the time of hearing;

(b) to assess the relative contributions of both the financial, non-financial, direct and indirect nature as specified by s 79(4);

(c) to consider the factors as are relevant contained in s 75(2) of the Family Law Act (“the Act”); and

(d) finally, consider the ultimate analysis to determine whether the order the Court proposes to make is just and equitable to both parties.

Both Counsel and the parties’ proposed orders make it clear that they both contend it is just and equitable to make orders (s 75(2)) and Stanford & Stanford [2012] HCA 52). I also agree.

COMPETING PROPOSALS

The wife, as articulated in her case outline filed 30 October 2024, largely relied on material she had prepared in anticipation of the first final hearing (which was unable to proceed but resulted in partial property Orders being made), with the addition of an updating Financial Statement and short affidavit. The wife relied upon her amended Initiating Application filed 28 November 2023, which in essence sought a division of the property assets in an amount equal to 55% of the nett assets to the wife and 45% to the husband. A mechanism to enable such division included provisions:
(a) for the husband to transfer to himself from his company G Pty Ltd:
(i) the sum of $1,385,000 to the wife; and

(ii) the portfolio of ASX listed shares to the value of $1,050,00 and transfer same to the wife.

(b) for the husband to transfer to the wife shares from his portfolio of ASX listed shares to the value of $1,172,952 (or such other sum to enable to wife to retain 55% of the nett assets).

At the commencement of the trial, the husband articulated his minute of final orders sought in his case outline filed on 30 October 2024 which sought a 60% adjustment of the property pool, in the husband’s favour. In facilitating this division, the husband proposes to pay actual taxation and realisation costs associated with the liquidation of assets held within the husband’s entities.
Following two days of cross-examination, at the commencement of oral submissions on the second day of the final hearing, the Applicant wife tendered an updated minute of final order, which is reproduced as Appendix One to these Reasons.
The Court expresses its regret to the parties in the delay in publishing these Reasons.

EVIDENCE IN RESPECT OF TAXATION ISSUES

The Court, by Order of 25 September 2024, appointed Vincents Chartered Accountants as a single expert to provide some opinions on three issues identified in their letter of instruction dated 3 October 2024, being:
What is the likely tax arising on the cash payment to Ms [Sowards] pursuant to Orders dated 16 December 2023;
What is the likely tax arising and transactional costs on the liquidation of assets so as [G] Pty Ltd can pay the tax payable for both:
(i) The years up to and including the year ending 30 June 2023; and
(ii) The year ending 30 June 2024 (given payment to Ms [Sowards] pursuant to the Order dated 6 December 2023); and
Tax arising and transactional costs of an in specie transfer for assets from [H] Pty Ltd and [G] Pty Ltd to Mr [Sowards] and the subsequent winding up of those two entities
The letter of instructions provided a “brief background”, which I regard as an agreed position or relevant history, and accordingly reproduce:
[G] Pty Ltd and [H] Pty Ltd
The Husband is the sole Director and 50% shareholder of the company [G] Pty Ltd;
The primary assets of [G] Pty Ltd is a property situated at [K] Street, [Suburb L] in the State of Queensland and shares held in publicly listed companies on the ASX.
The other 50% shareholder of [G] Pty Ltd is the company [H] Pty Ltd.
The Husband is the sole Director and 90% shareholder of [H] Pty Ltd and the primary assets of the company were shares traded on the ASX and its 50% interest in [G] Pty Ltd.
The other 10% shareholder of [H] Pty Ltd is the Husband's Mother, [Mrs O].

Cash payment to the Wife pursuant to Order made 6 December, 2023
By Order of the Court the Husband was to cause the Wife to be paid an interim payment of $700,000.
The Husband used funds held in the bank account of [G] Pty Ltd to meet the payment.

Tax for the years leading up to and including 30 June 2023
The Husband had not caused tax returns to be lodged for himself, [G] Pty Ltd or [H] Pty Ltd since 2001.
During the course of the proceedings the Husband has engaged and co-operated with the ATO to bring the tax affairs of himself and the two (2) entities up to the period ending 30 June, 2023.
[H Pty Ltd] receiving a tax refund of circa $31,703 which was directed by the Husband to be credited against the tax debt owed by [G] Pty Ltd;
Husband personally received a tax refund of circa $65,l02 which again he directed to be credited against the tax debt owed by [G] Pty Ltd; and
[G] Pty Ltd receiving an assessment to pay the ATO an amount, which after the credits referred to above being applied, as at 8 August, 2024 an amount of $728,809.74 was payable.
The tax due and payable by [G] Pty Ltd has been paid by the sale of shares held by the entity.
In effect, the nett taxes payable by the husband (after crediting refunds) was approximately $632,000 – which was paid by G Pty Ltd, by selling shares held by the entity.
Chartered Accountant Kim Reynolds, in her affidavit filed 5 November 2024, attached her initial report dated 4 November 2024, which was supplemented by a report dated 5 November 2024 (Exhibit 3). For the reasons given, the opinions of Ms Reynolds to the questions posed, in short were:
(a) because the husband caused the payment to the wife of $700,000 to be made directly from G Pty Ltd and is not treated as a dividend or repayment of a loan, it may be treated for tax purposes in one of three ways described at paragraph 2.9 of the first report. The view (supported by the husband’s accountants LL Financial Services) adopted is that the payment would be likely to be deemed a unfranked dividend, and would be subject to tax at the top marginal rate – amounting to $329,000 (paragraph 2.12) which was revised to an agreed figure of $321,700;

(b) in answer to question 2, where the tax liability was paid from the sale of shares (W Pty Ltd), such sale gave rise to a CGT event and an estimated tax liability of $241,897.80;

(c) in answer to question 3, and the tax arising and transactional costs of an in-specie transfer from the two entities to the husband and the subsequent winding up of those entities, Ms Reynolds opined that a transfer of assets will give rise to a CGT event – not only to shares held but significantly to the property at K Street, Suburb L held by G Pty Ltd. The estimated combined taxation liability amounts to $269,248 (paragraph 2.30) which assumed certain values at the time of transfer. The husband may be required to pay stamp duty on a transfer of the K Street property to him. The assumption is further made that “rollover” relief will not apply.

As to the second aspect of question 3, it was noted that various inter-entity loans on the balance sheet of the companies as at 30 June 2024 must be dealt with prior to winding up. The tax consequences are different depending on whether loans are repaid or forgiven; where Division 7A may apply (but not to loans between companies). However, for reasons explained at paragraph 2.52 of her first report, Ms Reynolds was unable to provide a “meaningful estimate of the taxation costs associated with winding up of the companies”.
As might have been expected, this first report caused further questions to be raised, answered in the supplementary report, as follows:
(a) Tax payable on the payment made to the wife of $700,000 would not now be relieved by trying to characterise the payment as a loan payable to the wife;

(b) No strategy to deal with the $700,000 payment was available that was more tax effective, because of the non-availability (at the time) of franking credits;

(c) Creatively, an option was raised about varying the order of December 2023 (by consent) to provide instead for G Pty Ltd to pay directly to the wife. No saving of tax was identified – in any event the Court has not been asked to vary the Orders;

(d) If final orders of the Court were to specify and require the in-specie transfer of assets to the husband, the CGT rollover relief outlined in paragraphs 2.34 to 2.36 of the first report should apply and provided the criteria in s 126.15 of the Income Tax Assessment Act 1997 (Cth) are satisfied. The tax liability upon transfer would be nil, with the husband inheriting the current cost based on the relevant assets as set out at paragraph 2.30 of the second report. However, Division 7A consequences may still arise; and

(e) Ms Reynolds was unable to provide an estimate as to the fluctuations in the example at paragraph 2.53 of the first report “given the number of variables that current exist”.

As was expected, these opinions and the number of uncertainties were explored in cross-examination, from which further evidence of relevance flowed, namely:
(a) the estimate at paragraph 2.53 of the first report is a “worse case scenario”, although depending on movements in loan accounts and sale of assets, that could impact the retained earnings. The impact could be different by “tens of thousands”, such that the estimate at paragraph 2.53 (of a total of $210,000) could be high;

(b) in further questioning of the expert by Counsel for the wife (seeking to clarify and quantify the potential tax implications), the expert and Counsel for the wife had this exchange:

Counsel: Right. In summary then, is it fair to say, in addition that worst case scenario of $210,000 that you have to for winding up the entities, if the husband was to transfer, pursuant to these Division 7A loan agreements that we’re talking about, the assets being the real property and shares held my [G Pty Ltd] and the assets held by [H Pty Ltd], being the shares, to himself personally and then wind up and deregister those companies. That would be further tax arising of around $550,000 if it was to occur in a single financial year?

Expert witness: If it was to occur in a single financial year and the in-specie distributions were treated as payments for Division 7A purposes, that’s correct.
(c) under the cross-examination that followed from Counsel for the husband, that all her calculations were based on a number of presumptions which may not be correct – and will only be clarified when draft financials are prepared, before actual lodgement with the ATO. That is the best time to properly calculate the taxation and winding up costs;

(d) when the Bench asked the expert about benefits, if for example the K Street property which the husband has held for over 30 years and now wants to retain and probably live in, was just kept in the entity, some tax benefits long term by the husband retaining the home in this name as a long term investment, but a transfer will trigger a capital gain payable by the entity. The expert agreed that, theoretically delaying the transfer over some years may be tax effective – at least it delays the crystallisation of the tax liabilities; and

(e) with further exploration and the effect of winding up the entities or not, Ms Reynolds, from a strategic tax advisor position, says the other options might be effective and she would go through a number of scenarios with the client.

POOL OF INTERESTS

A “joint balance sheet” (marked Exhibit 4) was tendered but subject to further submissions after the evidence was completed. As is apparent:
(a) most items were agreed and their value also accepted; and

(b) submissions as to various items (numbered as items in Exhibit 4 were made and considered).

Before making findings about issues in contest, I again note that I was not provided with financial statements for the entities but was invited to accept the value of interests of the husband in G Pty Ltd, H Pty Ltd and J Pty Ltd, based on agreed estimates of underlying assets. Inter party loans were, by agreement, excluded.
I note that in ascertaining the current value of real estate interests, single expert valuations were obtained and filed, and I infer accepted, as the experts were not required for cross-examination.
For completeness, neither party (both who were assisted by competent legal representation) have, since the case finished in November 2024, brought any application to adduce further evidence – including to “update” property valuations and/or share portfolio values and bank accounts. In these circumstances, I will proceed on the basis of Exhibit 4, shaped by the following further findings:
(a) One share in H Pty Ltd. The husband’s mother gave evidence that although she has held one share for many years, she regards that additional share as her son’s entitlement. In those circumstances, I adopt a value for the husband’s effective 10 shares in H Pty Ltd at $950,000;

(b) Properly, the wife brings into account in Exhibit 4 her partial property settlement, with her admission that she deposited $401,385 into her superannuation and a balance of $298,215 allowed for in the balance sheet;

(c) A dispute arises as to whether husband’s two shares in J Pty Ltd, which the wife says has a value of $270,000, should be included. As I understand the wife’s submission, this figure represents 50% of:

Shares (Exhibit 7)
	
$117,098

Suburb S Unit

(as per valuation of 28 October 2024)

	
$230,000

NN Bank Accounts
	
$130,000

	
$537,098
Say 50% = $270,000.

It is not disputed that the real property is registered in the name of J Pty Ltd – but not as Trustee (see Exhibit 2) and has been so registered since 1983 – 17 years before marriage. Whilst it is possible the company could own assets in its own right, the history of the Soward Family Trust earlier given in these Reasons strongly suggests that the company only acts as Trustee. The lack of financial statements for the Trust is unhelpful. However, the terms of the Trust create a discretionary trust, where the husband and his mother are the primary beneficiaries. Over the years, funds have been withdrawn for the benefit of both the husband and his mother. No distribution statements are available for the Trust. Considering the corpus of the Trust was created through the livestock farm and insurance, and giving weight to the evidence of Mrs O, I see no injustice to the wife in this case in excluding the value in the balance sheet, but taking it into account as a factor under s 75(2);

(d) The evidence of Ms Reynolds persuades me it is proper to bring into account a potential tax liability for the wife of $321,700 that seems to have arisen by the husband’s decision to pay the partial property settlement of $700,000 directly to the wife from G Pty Ltd. The Order made 6 December 2023 gave the husband seven days to take advice as to the manner in which he could source funds for the payment. In fact, I implored that he take advice (see [13] of my Reasons). It seems he did not do so. As my earlier Reasons set out, at December 2023, the husband had a share portfolio of $1,381,747 in his own name. He may have incurred some CGT personally by selling some shares, but his decision to pay from the company has created a liability – which both parties will share in essence, with the pool reduced by the liability. For a person like the husband who failed to lodge taxation returns for over 20 years, to do something to create a tax liability, rather than explore more tax effective options, is a total mystery to me;

(e) The balance sheet identified a dispute as to any potential tax liability associated with transferring assets out of the two corporate entities to the husband and further taxes on a winding up of the entities. At the conclusion of the evidence from Ms Reynolds, that tax could be as high as approximately $760,000. However, as I will explain later in these Reasons, such liabilities only arise if he seeks to transfer assets to himself and/or winds up the entities. In this respect, the major complication for the husband seems to be a desire now to transfer the Suburb L property from G Pty Ltd to himself, because of his wish to renovate (being substantial) because of its state of disrepair, so he can live in it – perhaps with one of the children. I would only try to incorporate a “figure” in the balance sheet if it was satisfied a liability will exist and only if its quantification had a degree of certainty. None of these factors are satisfied on the evidence, but that does not mean the potential liability should be ignored. Having heard submissions and considered the expert evidence, as unsatisfactory as it may be, I see no other option than to take these issues into consideration under s 75(2);

(f) Exhibit 4 excludes the wife’s asserted liability to her mother, said to be now $1,138,744 – most of which related to renovation costs on the wife’s Suburb D property of around $880,000 – performed post separation. The wife properly says this significant contribution was unmatched by the husband. I also note it was made later in the marriage and represents over 10% of the pool of interests. I deal with this submission below when discussing contributions. By not including the loan “owed” by the wife to her mother, the pool of interests is significantly increased by over 10%;

(g) In respect of legal fees, which continues to be a vexed question, the evidence demonstrates that the wife used loans from her mother and possibly some of the partial property distribution to pay her fees. The confusing position of the husband’s available resources, between his personal assets (primarily and still a substantive share portfolio), and cash and shares within the entities he controlled (together with the access from time to time to the Sowards Family Trust), means the available pool was reduced by him accessing these funds. I say this in circumstances where there is no evidence he has generated any income through personal exertion – rather he has had passive income through share dividends and capital accruals. Furthermore, the husband took no steps to improve his income by using the properties at Suburb S and Suburb L to generate income (although in respect of Suburb L it would have needed substantial outlays of funds to achieve a tenantable dwelling). In these circumstances, I agree with the submission of the wife’s Counsel that it is just to “add back” both the amounts identified as paid by the husband for his legal fees ($198,914) and the legal fees he paid for his elderly mother ($87,189) as a result of her engagement in the proceedings (see Trevi & Trevi [2018] FamCAFC 173). That engagement, which arose from an asserted belief of the wife that the P Street property (registered at all times in the name of Mrs O) ceased after Orders were made by me on 4 December 2023, when the wife’s application for declaratory relief relating to the P Street property was dismissed, and Mrs O was removed as a party; and

(h) As a result of these findings, and adopting concessions and agreement by the parties reflected in Exhibit 4, I find the pool of nett interests to total $7,623,811 as set out at Appendix Two to these Reasons.

CONTRIBUTIONS

In final submissions, both Counsel agreed that:
(a) the husband’s initial financial contributions were superior to those of the wife – although neither sought to “quantify” in dollar terms the difference. As the history earlier reveals, at cohabitation in 2000 the husband had purchased, it seems with the benefit of access to funds in the Sowards Family Trust, the property at GG Street (sold in 2001 for $450,00); his 50% interest in G Pty Ltd (which at least owned the property at K St Suburb L which is still retained); his interest in H Pty Ltd (which owned a 50% interest in G Pty Ltd) and shares in both his name personally and within the entities he controlled;

(b) the wife’s initial contributions included the B Street, Suburb D property (purchased in around 1994 for $205,000) which is still retained. Any modest mortgage of around $70,000 was discharged by the husband at the time or marriage. The wife also had modest superannuation entitlements accumulated during her employment as a health professional (such employment which was maintained at the time of the hearing);

(c) the parties resided in the Suburb D property owned by the wife from cohabitation until 2007 and then the family resided in the P Street property for 10 years (rent free) until final separation in 2017;

(d) during the marriage, the husband undertook property development but also continued to maintain and manage his share portfolio and other investments. The wife says, and I accept, that from around 2013 the husband ceased building developments, with more passively selling the last units under development. Although there was no medical evidence probatively offered to the Court, from around 2013 (I infer as the marriage started to develop issues), the husband’s mental health manifested in a way where procrastination and lack of focus became a feature. Whether by then this feature caused the husband to ignore his taxation responsibilities (he had not really filed tax returns since 2000) or whether those inabilities exacerbated his inability to do formal commercial functions, is impossible to say;

(e) the three children of the marriage were much loved, and I accept that although the wife was the primary homemaker and parent, the husband was an active parent, as his work pressures allowed (particularly difficult for him when managing the OO Street property in 2006);

(f) post separation, the wife managed major renovations to the B Street property, funded by loans from her mother totalling approximately $880,000; and

(g) whilst the husband contributed to the children’s maintenance (and at times Mr C has lived with the husband), I accept at the time of the hearing Mr C was spending time in the home of both parents. The evidence reveals that the wife received funds at times from her mother to assist in the payment of school fees, dental and orthodontic treatment for the children and other expenses – many of which were “added” to a loan the wife says she owes to her mother.

It must be acknowledged that, save for the substantial payments for taxation out of the G Pty Ltd entity, the steady increase in the value of the pool of interests arises from allowing the improving property market in Brisbane to create capital gains in the Suburb L property (of the husband) and the Suburb D property (of the wife). Certainly, the Suburb D property also benefited in its market value from significant renovations. Overall, the husband’s management of his share portfolios appears to be by adopting a style of buying quality stock and holding that stock – either receiving dividends or reinvesting the dividends. He did not seem to be an active “share trader” – with the best example of this investment style demonstrated by purchasing shares in W Pty Ltd for $874 and selling them for $807,2000.
With little dispute on the direct and indirect financial and non-financial contributions made by the parties during the relationship – and understanding the principles requiring proper recognition of how initial contributions must be weighed with the other myriad of contributions (including as homemaker and parent) in a long relationship (see Pierce & Pierce (1998) FLC 92-844) and further being conscious of the need to undertake a holistic assessment (see Jabour & Jabour [2019] FamCAFC 78), both experienced Counsel’s final submissions on contributions revealed a disparity:
(a) with the husband contending for a 52.5%/47.5% adjustment in the husband’s favour; and

(b) with the wife contending for a 52.5%/47.5% adjustment in the in the wife’s favour.

In my assessment of the contributions as identified, the late contribution of $880,000 to renovations by the wife causes me to find contribution based entitlements were 51.5% to the wife and 48.5% to the husband – a differential of 3% or a figure on a pool of $7,623,811 of approximately $236,000.

SECTION 75(2) FACTORS

In final submissions, attention by Counsel for the parties was focussed on three issues, in circumstances where:
(a) the age difference between the parties (the husband being 10 years older) means that the wife’s earning capacity and future income as a health professional will continue for some years (as well as her needs), whilst the husband will rely upon his income generated by rentals, interest on capital or dividends. The parties claimed a similar current gross income per week are similar;

(b) the youngest child X is now 16 years of age and lives almost on an equal time basis whilst the older children – Ms CC now aged 23 years lives with the wife, and Mr C now aged 21 years old lives with the husband – as adults, whilst generally supported in different ways by their parents, they are required to support themselves;

(c) the wife will retain a superannuation interest of over $800,000 but swelled by her decision to contribute over $400,000 of the partial property settlement to her superannuation which I assume was based on financial advice. As the wife is now aged 55 years, options to access her superannuation exist through a transition to retirement pension and ultimately to withdraw the entity of her benefit, tax free; and

(d) the orders for property adjustment mean that each party will control significant capital or property and have choices as to how, in the future, they manage their resources.

These factors would not compel an adjustment to contribution based entitlements. However, the following facts do require consideration of an adjustment:
(a) In circumstances where the legal interest of Mrs O in the P Street property is not disputed, I accept the husband has had the benefit of living in that home for over eight years post separation. The evidence of his mother, which I accept, is that she now intends to sell the property and the husband will need to vacate the home. I also accept the husband’s interest as a discretionary beneficiary under the terms of the Sowards Family Trust could accrue some benefit in the future – but the corpus of the Trust is modest, and history shows both Mrs O and the husband have benefited from distributions in the past, in differing amounts;

(b) A bigger adjustment factor arises from the wife’s interest under the testamentary instruments created by the late Mr N. The husband asserts, and some cross-examination was directed to the wife’s failure in a timely way to make full and complete disclosure about her interest in what was collectively called the DD Trust. I am satisfied that the wife did not initially make full disclosure, but by the hearing before me, and after a subpoena resulted in production of documents in April 2024 and August 2024, I am satisfied the nature and extent of the wife’s interest of 25% has been established. To this end, the wife was not seriously challenged on the evidence at paragraphs 145 to 152 of her affidavit filed 14 November 2023, where the wife admitted her interest is a “financial resource” and, at that time, calculated her interest to be contingently $866,236.25 (paragraph 149). It is not in dispute that the wife’s mother (who is in her early 80s) has a life interest and entitlement to the income of the Trust, and upon her death, the wife, as one of the four children, will then inherit;

(c) In final submissions, Counsel for the husband maintained some uncertainty dispute discovery having occurred, exists as to the actual current value of the corpus of the Trust, whilst Counsel for the wife asserted that the Court should accept as accurate, the wife’s evidence at paragraph 64 of her affidavit filed 16 October 2024, that the current corpus of the Trust is $2,697,362 whereby the wife’s potential 25% share amounts to $674,340. I take this evidence into account;

(d) A matter which was not really the subject of submission, is the manner in which any future taxation obligations might require payment by the husband. As the evidence of Ms Reynolds makes clear, much depends on how the husband chooses to manage his affairs – hopefully and maybe for the first time) after proper taking advice. When Mr Alexander asserted it would be potentially unfair to include a fixed sum for tax liabilities in the balance sheet because of the numerous uncertainties, that submission was attractive. I note, doing the best she could, that Ms Horsley for the wife sought a fixed amount but had to concede the uncertainties that exist. Doing the best I can, I am satisfied that some future tax liabilities may be incurred by the husband, which will depend upon the steps he takes to deal with his corporate entities; how, if at all, he chooses to have his Suburb L property transferred from the company to himself personally; and whether the orders I make compel winding up the entities. As will be revealed, I do not propose to make such orders to wind up;

(e) The wife submits that, as I understand her contention in her case outline, that s 75(2)(o) should be used to make an adjustment because of the husband’s conduct in these proceedings “and the impact of that conduct on the wife”. At paragraph 157 of her affidavit filed on 16 November 2023, the wife referred to the husband’s failure to progress his obligation to file tax returns, both before proceedings commenced and thereafter (often including a failure to comply with directions). The crystallisation of the tax liabilities was achieved and is reflected in the reduced pool – but where those debts accumulated during the marriage, the payment (without it seems penalties and interest being incurred) is not a matter the wife can now dispute. In my view, issues about how the wife’s engagement in the legal proceedings have been the cause of her incurring further costs, are best dealt with in the context of a costs application if pressed; and

(f) By excluding a debt to the wife’s mother in the balance sheet (by agreement), I do not ignore some responsibility the wife might have to either repay the debt or to somehow adjust with her siblings. No evidence from the wife’s mother meant it was hard to make findings as to her intention to recover (see Biltoft & Biltoft [1995] FamCAFC 45; (1995) FLC 92-614).

Taking all these factors into account, I am satisfied an adjustment in the husband’s favour of 2% is appropriate – an overall difference of 4% or on the pool of $7,623,811 a sum of approximately $305,000.

WHAT ORDERS ACHIEVE JUSTICE AND EQUITY – AND FINALITY?

Based on the earlier analysis, a division of the pool of interests as to 50.5% to the husband and 49.5% to the wife may be appropriate, but of course it is the nature of the orders which must be considered when determining whether justice and equity is achieved.
Of course, the terms of the partial property orders are only relevant historically, although the payment to the wife of $700,000 (and the incidental taxation) is incorporated in the current identified pool of interests.
The “notional” pool adopted in the Reasons for Judgment in December 2023 is very different from that which exists now, in particular:
(a) the husband’s corporate interests totalling an estimated $3,830,150 have now reduced (as agreed – save for the shares in J Pty Ltd) to $1,767,000. Even allowing for the approximately $1,330,000 withdrawn from G Pty Ltd to pay the wife $700,000 and to pay tax of $630,000, the reduction in the corporate entities (as agreed in Exhibit 4) must reflect a better estimate now. Where the Court has no financial statements, the Court feels bound by the parties’ agreement; and

(b) the husband’s personal share portfolio has increased by $370,000 over its value of $1,381,747 in December 2023; and

(c) the estimate of potential tax liabilities in December 2023 of $1,000,000 was overestimated; and

(d) the wife no longer seeks to include the “loan” from her mother of $881,204 (at December 2023).

If the wife was to receive 49.5% of the current pool of $7,623,811, this amounts to $3,773,786 made up of:

In my assessment, the husband paying to the wife the sum of $193,500 (rounded up) within 60 days will achieve justice and equity.
I accept he may have to source the payment from the sale of shares, this incurring possibly CGT, but that is likely to be modest (if at all).
I am somewhat hamstrung in this matter by:
(a) the lack of any reliable financial statements for the entities and trusts; and

(b) limited evidence on behalf of the husband to show how he may be able, in a tax effective way, to simply his affairs.

There is however no need, in view of what is the payment due from the husband, to order a liquidation or winding up of any entities controlled by the husband. If he, on advice, chooses to do so, then that is a matter for him. The Suburb L property has been held in the corporate entity for 30 years – the Suburb S property even longer. Further, I see no reason to order the husband to file further personal or corporate tax returns as the wife proposed. If after his experience the husband has an appetite to pay more income tax (or incur penalties for failing to file), there is nothing the Court can do.
The wife is entitled to finality, although taking a broad-brush approach, with all the uncertainties that this matter has experienced, of an end result of almost equality feels fair. I note that both parties are likely to have:
(a) benefits from their mothers or arising from their mothers’ death in the future;

(b) little debt exists (save for the wife’s debt to her mother);

(c) the wife has a future income and sizeable superannuation available to her; and

(d) the husband, with proper advice, if he chooses to do so, can simplify his affairs, and with his tax issues now in order, build on the capital and assets he controls.

For these reasons, the orders which appear at the commencement of these Reasons are just and equitable to both parties.
